metadata.summary: Test doubles (stubs, mocks, spies, fakes) isolate code under test from external dependencies. Use the smallest double that validates the required behavior.

content.title: Mocking Strategies
content.summary: Test doubles (stubs, mocks, spies, fakes) isolate code under test from external dependencies. Use the smallest double that validates the required behavior: fake for repository abstractions, stub for state return values, mock only when the call itself is part of the contract, spy to wrap real objects.

why.p: Tests that reach real databases, HTTP services, clocks, or random sources are slow, flaky, and environment-dependent. Test doubles replace these collaborators with deterministic stand-ins. Choosing the wrong double (e.g., mocking everything) produces tests that pass even when the production code is broken — mutation testing reveals this. Mock only at architectural seams.

list.item: Isolating unit tests from databases, HTTP services, filesystems, clocks, or randomness
Testing error handling and edge cases by controlling what a dependency returns
Verifying that a specific interaction (e.g., email sent on order placement) occurred
Replacing slow I/O with instant in-memory fakes for the service layer
Writing characterization tests around legacy code by stubbing its dependencies
Integration tests that should hit a real DB/queue/cache — use Testcontainers instead
Pure functions — they need no mocks; mocking their inputs reveals a design smell
Tests of the boundary itself (the HTTP client, the SQL layer) — test against real or VCR-recorded backend
End-to-end tests — should exercise real services in a controlled environment
When a 30-line FakeRepository is cheaper to maintain than setting up mocks per test

rule.statement: Choose the smallest double that validates the required behavior: dummy for unused params, stub for state, spy for wrapped real objects, mock only when the call is part of the contract, fake for full working replacements.
rule.rationale: Using a mock where a stub suffices couples the test to implementation details. Mutation testing reveals over-mocked suites: tests that pass even when production code is broken.

list.item: DUMMY — passed but never used; fills required parameter slots
STUB — returns canned responses; tests state, not behavior
SPY — wraps a real object; records calls for later verification
MOCK — pre-programmed with expectations; verifies interactions
FAKE — working in-memory implementation (e.g., FakeRepository)

example.title: Python stub and mock
example.code:
from unittest.mock import Mock import pytest
# STUB — controls return value, tests state def test_get_user_returns_name(): repo = Mock() repo.find_by_id.return_value = User(id="123", name="John") service = UserService(repo) result = service.get_user("123") assert result.name == "John"
# MOCK — verifies that the call happened (part of contract) def test_order_placed_sends_notification(): notifier = Mock() service = OrderService(notifier=notifier) service.place_order(order_id="order-123") notifier.send_notification.assert_called_once_with( "order-123", "Your order has been placed" )
# SPY — wraps real object, call passes through def test_spy_records_call(): real_calc = Calculator() spy = Mock(wraps=real_calc) result = spy.add(2, 3) assert result == 5 # real behavior spy.add.assert_called_with(2, 3)

example.title: Python fake repository
example.note: A 30-line fake survives refactoring and is reused across the entire test suite. Prefer fakes over mocks for repository abstractions.

example.title: TypeScript Jest mocks

rule.statement: Patch where the name is looked up, not where it is defined. If module_a imports function_b from module_b and you test module_a, patch 'module_a.function_b'.
rule.rationale: Patching the source module leaves the import alias in the module under test untouched; the mock never takes effect.

example.title: Patch forms

rule.statement: Use AsyncMock for any function defined with async def. Using plain Mock for async functions causes await to return a coroutine wrapping a Mock, not the mock's return value.
rule.rationale: AsyncMock automatically awaits and returns return_value; plain Mock does not participate in the async protocol correctly.

example.title: AsyncMock usage
Side effects

antipattern.description: Mocking the system under test rather than its dependencies.
antipattern.reason: If you mock the function being tested, you're not testing anything. The mock always passes regardless of whether the real implementation works.

antipattern.description: Using Mock() instead of Mock(spec=RealClass) or autospec=True.
antipattern.reason: Plain Mock returns a Mock for any attribute access, including misspelled names. spec= or autospec=True raises AttributeError on typos, catching bugs immediately.

antipattern.description: Over-mocking: mocking internal helpers and verifying every internal call.
antipattern.reason: Tests coupled to implementation details break on every refactor, even when behavior is unchanged. Verify only calls that are part of the external contract.

antipattern.description: Not resetting mocks between tests (state leaking across tests).
antipattern.reason: Call counts and return values accumulate. Use pytest-mock's mocker fixture (auto-resets) or explicit mock.reset_mock() in setUp/tearDown.

antipattern.description: Mocking data structures (dataclasses, Pydantic models) instead of using real instances.
antipattern.reason: Mocking value objects defeats type safety and usually signals the production design needs adjustment, not the test.

antipattern.description: Using @patch with wrong target path (patching source module, not lookup site).
antipattern.reason: @patch('module_b.function_b') when module_a imports function_b leaves module_a's local name unaffected; the test exercises the real code.

rule.statement: Keep mock setup in fixtures, assertions in test functions. Long inline mock setup (more than 5 lines) in a test body is a smell indicating either too many collaborators or a design problem.
rule.rationale: Fixture-based setup is reusable and keeps tests readable. If setup needs more than 5 lines, the system under test probably has too many dependencies.

list.item: Mock at architectural seams (repositories, gateways, clients), not internal helpers.
Prefer fakes over mocks for repositories — a 30-line FakeUserRepository is reusable across the suite and survives refactors.
Use assert_called_with(...) only for behavior that is part of the contract (e.g., "an email is sent on order placement"); not for incidental calls.
For HTTP mocks, prefer route-based matchers (URL + method + body shape) over exact-string matches; resilient to harmless changes.
Reset mocks between tests (pytest-mock does this automatically; raw unittest.mock does not unless using context managers or autouse fixtures).
For time, prefer injecting a Clock interface and providing a FakeClock fake over freeze_time; cleaner and faster.
Keep mock setup in fixtures, assertions in tests — long inline mock setup is a smell.

section.name: agent-workflow
section.p: Make the agent choose the right test double first, write the test second. Use a two-step prompt: (1) given the production code, classify each external collaborator as pure | stub | fake | mock | integration; (2) generate the test using the chosen double. This prevents the default LLM behavior of mocking everything. Run mutation tests (mutmut, cosmic-ray) periodically to catch over-mocked tests that pass even when production code is broken.

list.item: pytest-mock — mocker fixture wrapping unittest.mock
freezegun — Freeze time for deterministic tests
time-machine — Faster freezegun alternative
responses — Stub requests calls
pytest-httpx — Stub httpx calls
respx — httpx mocking, route-based
vcrpy — Record/replay real HTTP traffic
wiremock — Standalone HTTP mock server
msw (Mock Service Worker) — TS/JS network mocking, browser+node
nock — Node HTTP interceptor
sinon — JS spies/stubs/mocks
mutmut / cosmic-ray — Mutation testing — catches over-mocked suites
Agents instinctively mock the function under test rather than its dependencies. Add an explicit "do not mock the system under test" rule.
@patch('foo.bar.baz') patches the import location, not the source. Agents patch the source (baz's defining module), which doesn't take effect. Always patch where it's looked up.
Mock() returns a Mock for any attribute access — typos in attribute names silently pass. Use spec=RealClass or autospec=True to get errors instead.
AsyncMock vs Mock confusion: agent mocks an async function with Mock and await returns a coroutine wrapping a Mock. Use AsyncMock always for async def.
Agents over-use side_effect lists; if the production code calls the dependency more times than expected, you get StopIteration instead of a clear error.
Mocking dataclasses or pydantic models almost always means the design needs adjustment, not the test. Reject these.
freeze_time on a function decorator does NOT propagate to subprocesses or threads; agents misuse this in concurrency tests.
Human-in-loop checkpoint: review any test that mocks more than two collaborators per test function — this is the over-mocking smell threshold.

template.purpose: In-memory fake repository implementing an ABC interface
Script: flags test functions with too many assert_called (over-mocking detector)
Two-step prompt: classify collaborators then generate test using the right double
